messages.text:
Nazywam się Monika Śmietana i jestem dietetykiem klinicznym oraz specjalistą żywienia dzieci. Moim zamiłowaniem jest medycyna żywieniowa. Pomagam zrozumieć zdrowym i chorym pacjentom jak pozytywny wpływ na ich życie mają dobre nawyki żywieniowe. 
DZIECKO POWINNO BYĆ ODŻYWIANE, A NIE TYLKO KARMIONE
Jako mama oraz dietetyk dziecięcy uważam, że zdrowo odżywiane dziecko otrzymuje od rodziców lepszy start w przyszłość, prawidłowy rozwój fizyczny oraz intelektualny. Prowadzę edukuję żywieniową dla rodziców dzieci z trudnościami w żywieniu. Pomagam rodzinom kształtować prawidłowe nawyki żywieniowe, już od początku rozszerzania diety ich dzieci.

NAJLEPSZA DIETA, TO TA KTÓRĄ JESTEŚ W STANIE REALIZOWAĆ
Do swoich pacjentów podchodzę indywidualnie. Przy układaniu programu żywieniowego biorę pod uwagę czynniki takie jak: choroby, rodzaj aktywności, preferencje smakowe i umiejętności kulinarne. Kładę nacisk na to aby produkty wchodzące w skład jadłospisów były łatwo dostępne i dopasowane do każdego budżetu.

Nieustannie podnoszę swoje kwalifikacje poprzez udział w kursach i szkoleniach. Staram się docierać do rzetelnych i aktualnych badań naukowych.
Swoją opieką obejmuję kobiety w ciąży i karmiące, biorę pod uwagę nierzadkie schorzenia, które w tym czasie mogą występować. 
Pomagam pacjentom kardiologicznym, chorym na otyłość, nadwagę i niedowagę, cierpiącym na choroby dietozależne, autoimmunologiczne.

Jeśli czujesz, że mogę Ci pomóc - skontaktuj się ze mną!

text_entities.text:
Nazywam się Monika Śmietana i jestem dietetykiem klinicznym oraz specjalistą żywienia dzieci. Moim zamiłowaniem jest medycyna żywieniowa. Pomagam zrozumieć zdrowym i chorym pacjentom jak pozytywny wpływ na ich życie mają dobre nawyki żywieniowe. 
DZIECKO POWINNO BYĆ ODŻYWIANE, A NIE TYLKO KARMIONE
Jako mama oraz dietetyk dziecięcy uważam, że zdrowo odżywiane dziecko otrzymuje od rodziców lepszy start w przyszłość, prawidłowy rozwój fizyczny oraz intelektualny. Prowadzę edukuję żywieniową dla rodziców dzieci z trudnościami w żywieniu. Pomagam rodzinom kształtować prawidłowe nawyki żywieniowe, już od początku rozszerzania diety ich dzieci.

NAJLEPSZA DIETA, TO TA KTÓRĄ JESTEŚ W STANIE REALIZOWAĆ
Do swoich pacjentów podchodzę indywidualnie. Przy układaniu programu żywieniowego biorę pod uwagę czynniki takie jak: choroby, rodzaj aktywności, preferencje smakowe i umiejętności kulinarne. Kładę nacisk na to aby produkty wchodzące w skład jadłospisów były łatwo dostępne i dopasowane do każdego budżetu.

Nieustannie podnoszę swoje kwalifikacje poprzez udział w kursach i szkoleniach. Staram się docierać do rzetelnych i aktualnych badań naukowych.
Swoją opieką obejmuję kobiety w ciąży i karmiące, biorę pod uwagę nierzadkie schorzenia, które w tym czasie mogą występować. 
Pomagam pacjentom kardiologicznym, chorym na otyłość, nadwagę i niedowagę, cierpiącym na choroby dietozależne, autoimmunologiczne.

Jeśli czujesz, że mogę Ci pomóc - skontaktuj się ze mną!

# MicroPython SSD1306 OLED driver, I2C and SPI interfaces

from micropython import const
import framebuf


# register definitions
SET_CONTRAST = const(0x81)
SET_ENTIRE_ON = const(0xA4)
SET_NORM_INV = const(0xA6)
SET_DISP = const(0xAE)
SET_MEM_ADDR = const(0x20)
SET_COL_ADDR = const(0x21)
SET_PAGE_ADDR = const(0x22)
SET_DISP_START_LINE = const(0x40)
SET_SEG_REMAP = const(0xA0)
SET_MUX_RATIO = const(0xA8)
SET_COM_OUT_DIR = const(0xC0)
SET_DISP_OFFSET = const(0xD3)
SET_COM_PIN_CFG = const(0xDA)
SET_DISP_CLK_DIV = const(0xD5)
SET_PRECHARGE = const(0xD9)
SET_VCOM_DESEL = const(0xDB)
SET_CHARGE_PUMP = const(0x8D)

# Subclassing FrameBuffer provides support for graphics primitives
#

(self, width, height, external_vcc):
        self.width = width
        self.height = height
        self.external_vcc = external_vcc
        self.pages = self.height // 8
        self.buffer = bytearray(self.pages * self.width)
        super().

(self.buffer, self.width, self.height, framebuf.MONO_VLSB)
        self.init_display()

    def init_display(self):
        for cmd in (
            SET_DISP | 0x00,  # off
            # address setting
            SET_MEM_ADDR,
            0x00,  # horizontal
            # resolution and layout
            SET_DISP_START_LINE | 0x00,
            SET_SEG_REMAP | 0x01,  # column addr 127 mapped to SEG0
            SET_MUX_RATIO,
            self.height - 1,
            SET_COM_OUT_DIR | 0x08,  # scan from COM[N] to COM0
            SET_DISP_OFFSET,
            0x00,
            SET_COM_PIN_CFG,
            0x02 if self.width > 2 * self.height else 0x12,
            # timing and driving scheme
            SET_DISP_CLK_DIV,
            0x80,
            SET_PRECHARGE,
            0x22 if self.external_vcc else 0xF1,
            SET_VCOM_DESEL,
            0x30,  # 0.83*Vcc
            # display
            SET_CONTRAST,
            0xFF,  # maximum
            SET_ENTIRE_ON,  # output follows RAM contents
            SET_NORM_INV,  # not inverted
            # charge pump
            SET_CHARGE_PUMP,
            0x10 if self.external_vcc else 0x14,
            SET_DISP | 0x01,
        ):  # on
            self.write_cmd(cmd)
        self.fill(0)

()

    def poweroff(self):
        self.write_cmd(SET_DISP | 0x00)

    def poweron(self):
        self.write_cmd(SET_DISP | 0x01)

    def contrast(self, contrast):
        self.write_cmd(SET_CONTRAST)
        self.write_cmd(contrast)

    def invert(self, invert):
        self.write_cmd(SET_NORM_INV | (invert & 1))

    def show(self):
        x0 = 0
        x1 = self.width - 1
        if self.width == 64:
            # displays with width of 64 pixels are shifted by 32
            x0 += 32
            x1 += 32
        self.write_cmd(SET_COL_ADDR)
        self.write_cmd(x0)
        self.write_cmd(x1)
        self.write_cmd(SET_PAGE_ADDR)
        self.write_cmd(0)
        self.write_cmd(self.pages - 1)
        self.write_data(self.buffer)


class SSD1306_I2C(SSD1306):
    def

(self, width, height, i2c, addr=0x3C, external_vcc=False):
        self.i2c = i2c
        self.addr = addr
        self.temp = bytearray(2)
        self.write_list = [b"\x40", None]  # Co=0, D/C#=1
        super().

(self, width, height, spi, dc, res, cs, external_vcc=False):
        self.rate = 10 * 1024 * 1024
        dc.init(dc.OUT, value=0)
        res.init(res.OUT, value=0)
        cs.init(cs.OUT, value=1)
        self.spi = spi
        self.dc = dc
        self.res = res
        self.cs = cs
        import time

# MicroPython SSD1306 OLED driver, I2C and SPI interfaces

from micropython import const
import framebuf


# register definitions
SET_CONTRAST = const(0x81)
SET_ENTIRE_ON = const(0xA4)
SET_NORM_INV = const(0xA6)
SET_DISP = const(0xAE)
SET_MEM_ADDR = const(0x20)
SET_COL_ADDR = const(0x21)
SET_PAGE_ADDR = const(0x22)
SET_DISP_START_LINE = const(0x40)
SET_SEG_REMAP = const(0xA0)
SET_MUX_RATIO = const(0xA8)
SET_COM_OUT_DIR = const(0xC0)
SET_DISP_OFFSET = const(0xD3)
SET_COM_PIN_CFG = const(0xDA)
SET_DISP_CLK_DIV = const(0xD5)
SET_PRECHARGE = const(0xD9)
SET_VCOM_DESEL = const(0xDB)
SET_CHARGE_PUMP = const(0x8D)

# Subclassing FrameBuffer provides support for graphics primitives
#

(self, width, height, external_vcc):
        self.width = width
        self.height = height
        self.external_vcc = external_vcc
        self.pages = self.height // 8
        self.buffer = bytearray(self.pages * self.width)
        super().

(self.buffer, self.width, self.height, framebuf.MONO_VLSB)
        self.init_display()

    def init_display(self):
        for cmd in (
            SET_DISP | 0x00,  # off
            # address setting
            SET_MEM_ADDR,
            0x00,  # horizontal
            # resolution and layout
            SET_DISP_START_LINE | 0x00,
            SET_SEG_REMAP | 0x01,  # column addr 127 mapped to SEG0
            SET_MUX_RATIO,
            self.height - 1,
            SET_COM_OUT_DIR | 0x08,  # scan from COM[N] to COM0
            SET_DISP_OFFSET,
            0x00,
            SET_COM_PIN_CFG,
            0x02 if self.width > 2 * self.height else 0x12,
            # timing and driving scheme
            SET_DISP_CLK_DIV,
            0x80,
            SET_PRECHARGE,
            0x22 if self.external_vcc else 0xF1,
            SET_VCOM_DESEL,
            0x30,  # 0.83*Vcc
            # display
            SET_CONTRAST,
            0xFF,  # maximum
            SET_ENTIRE_ON,  # output follows RAM contents
            SET_NORM_INV,  # not inverted
            # charge pump
            SET_CHARGE_PUMP,
            0x10 if self.external_vcc else 0x14,
            SET_DISP | 0x01,
        ):  # on
            self.write_cmd(cmd)
        self.fill(0)

()

    def poweroff(self):
        self.write_cmd(SET_DISP | 0x00)

    def poweron(self):
        self.write_cmd(SET_DISP | 0x01)

    def contrast(self, contrast):
        self.write_cmd(SET_CONTRAST)
        self.write_cmd(contrast)

    def invert(self, invert):
        self.write_cmd(SET_NORM_INV | (invert & 1))

    def show(self):
        x0 = 0
        x1 = self.width - 1
        if self.width == 64:
            # displays with width of 64 pixels are shifted by 32
            x0 += 32
            x1 += 32
        self.write_cmd(SET_COL_ADDR)
        self.write_cmd(x0)
        self.write_cmd(x1)
        self.write_cmd(SET_PAGE_ADDR)
        self.write_cmd(0)
        self.write_cmd(self.pages - 1)
        self.write_data(self.buffer)


class SSD1306_I2C(SSD1306):
    def

(self, width, height, i2c, addr=0x3C, external_vcc=False):
        self.i2c = i2c
        self.addr = addr
        self.temp = bytearray(2)
        self.write_list = [b"\x40", None]  # Co=0, D/C#=1
        super().

(self, width, height, spi, dc, res, cs, external_vcc=False):
        self.rate = 10 * 1024 * 1024
        dc.init(dc.OUT, value=0)
        res.init(res.OUT, value=0)
        cs.init(cs.OUT, value=1)
        self.spi = spi
        self.dc = dc
        self.res = res
        self.cs = cs
        import time

text:
self.res(1)
        time.sleep_ms(1)
        self.res(0)
        time.sleep_ms(10)
        self.res(1)
        super().

(width, height, external_vcc)

    def write_cmd(self, cmd):
        self.spi.init(baudrate=self.rate, polarity=0, phase=0)
        self.cs(1)
        self.dc(0)
        self.cs(0)
        self.spi.write(bytearray([cmd]))
        self.cs(1)

    def write_data(self, buf):
        self.spi.init(baudrate=self.rate, polarity=0, phase=0)
        self.cs(1)
        self.dc(1)
        self.cs(0)
        self.spi.write(buf)
        self.cs(1)

        time.sleep_ms(1)

        super().

(width, height, external_vcc)

    def write_cmd(self, cmd):
        self.spi.init(baudrate=self.rate, polarity=0, phase=0)
        self.cs(1)
        self.dc(0)
        self.cs(0)
        self.spi.write(bytearray([cmd]))
        self.cs(1)

    def write_data(self, buf):
        self.spi.init(baudrate=self.rate, polarity=0, phase=0)
        self.cs(1)
        self.dc(1)
        self.cs(0)
        self.spi.write(buf)
        self.cs(1)

messages.text:
I am a web developer with 14+ years of experience. Today I'm having a call with 2 most important persons in the agency I work for and the client I've been working for via this agency for the past 2 years. The client guy is kinda pricky, arrogant, often acting childish like quitting the call after making a bold statement, giving 0 time for the team to respond or ask. On the other hand he's not very remembering and he forgets most of these statements. But recently I'm feeling his negativity towards me. I think he wants to end cooperation with me but he don't have balls to make it in a clean way. Recently he's been going through AI-sickness and he yells here and there that he wants more AI, that all should be done via AI. His arguments are silly and well known already in the world - he made a couple of non-interactive wireframes and thinks making the same stuff but within a complex, 15+ years old legacy environment he manages should be done as easily and he's 100% sure it needs to be done like that and that it will. Help me to prepare arguments to confront his madness in a good manner. The agency team is on my side - if he wants to get rid of me he will have it, but I want to make him look stupid during that call.

You’re walking into a politically charged meeting, not a technical meeting. That distinction matters more than any AI argument.

Your instinct is to “make him look stupid,” but that will likely backfire. If he’s arrogant, impulsive, and status-driven, public embarrassment usually makes people double down and attack harder. The smarter move is to make his position look incomplete while making you look calm, senior, and solution-oriented.

Primary Objective
Don’t win an argument. Win the room.

That means:

Stay composed.

Speak in business terms, not ego terms.

Show you understand AI and delivery reality.

Force specificity.

Make risk visible without sounding defensive.

If the agency is already on your side, you don’t need theatrics. You need contrast.

Core Framing for the Call
Position yourself as:
Experienced

Practical

Open to AI

Focused on outcomes

Honest about constraints

Position him indirectly as:
Oversimplifying

Asking without prioritization

Ignoring implementation realities

Mixing prototypes with production systems

You do this through questions and structure, not insults.

Best Arguments to Use
1. “AI is a tool, not a delivery strategy.”
Say:

“I’m absolutely in favor of using AI where it creates leverage—code generation, refactoring support, documentation, test scaffolding, analysis. But AI doesn’t remove architecture constraints, integration complexity, security requirements, or legacy dependencies.”

This prevents him painting you as anti-AI.

2. Prototype ≠ Production
Say:

“Creating wireframes or mock flows is fast because there’s no production data, edge cases, permissions, regressions, deployment pipeline, historical debt, or user behavior to support. Shipping inside a 15-year-old live system is a different category of work.”

That’s devastating because it’s true.

3. Ask for measurable outcomes
Say:

“When we say ‘more AI,’ what exact business outcome are we targeting—faster delivery, lower cost, fewer bugs, better UX, automation of support, internal productivity?”

If he can’t answer, he sounds vague.

4. Request prioritization
Say:

“There are many ways to apply AI. Which three opportunities have the highest ROI for this business right now?”

Now he must choose instead of rant.

5. Risk framing
Say:

“Moving fast is fine. Moving fast in the wrong direction creates expensive rework. I’d rather identify where AI adds real value than bolt it onto everything because it’s trendy.”

Strong but professional.

If He Says “AI Can Build This Easily”
Say:

“If by ‘build’ we mean generate a demo, yes often quickly. If we mean production-ready, maintainable, secure, integrated, observable, tested software inside an aging environment—that’s a different standard.”

If He Attacks You Personally or Implies Resistance
Say calmly:

The client guy is kinda pricky, arrogant, often acting childish like quitting the call after making a bold statement, giving 0 time for the team to respond or ask. On the other hand he's not very remembering and he forgets most of these statements. But recently I'm feeling his negativity towards me. I think he wants to end cooperation with me but he don't have balls to make it in a clean way. Recently he's been going through AI-sickness and he yells here and there that he wants more AI, that all should be done via AI. His arguments are silly and well known already in the world - he made a couple of non-interactive wireframes and thinks making the same stuff but within a complex, 15+ years old legacy environment he manages should be done as easily and he's 100% sure it needs to be done like that and that it will. Help me to prepare arguments to confront his madness in a good manner. The agency team is on my side - if he wants to get rid of me he will have it, but I want to make him look stupid during that call.

You’re walking into a politically charged meeting, not a technical meeting. That distinction matters more than any AI argument.

Your instinct is to “make him look stupid,” but that will likely backfire. If he’s arrogant, impulsive, and status-driven, public embarrassment usually makes people double down and attack harder. The smarter move is to make his position look incomplete while making you look calm, senior, and solution-oriented.

Primary Objective
Don’t win an argument. Win the room.

That means:

Stay composed.

Speak in business terms, not ego terms.

Show you understand AI and delivery reality.

Force specificity.

Make risk visible without sounding defensive.

If the agency is already on your side, you don’t need theatrics. You need contrast.

Core Framing for the Call
Position yourself as:
Experienced

Practical

Open to AI

Focused on outcomes

Honest about constraints

Position him indirectly as:
Oversimplifying

Asking without prioritization

Ignoring implementation realities

Mixing prototypes with production systems

You do this through questions and structure, not insults.

Best Arguments to Use
1. “AI is a tool, not a delivery strategy.”
Say:

“I’m absolutely in favor of using AI where it creates leverage—code generation, refactoring support, documentation, test scaffolding, analysis. But AI doesn’t remove architecture constraints, integration complexity, security requirements, or legacy dependencies.”

This prevents him painting you as anti-AI.

2. Prototype ≠ Production
Say:

“Creating wireframes or mock flows is fast because there’s no production data, edge cases, permissions, regressions, deployment pipeline, historical debt, or user behavior to support. Shipping inside a 15-year-old live system is a different category of work.”

That’s devastating because it’s true.

3. Ask for measurable outcomes
Say:

“When we say ‘more AI,’ what exact business outcome are we targeting—faster delivery, lower cost, fewer bugs, better UX, automation of support, internal productivity?”

If he can’t answer, he sounds vague.

4. Request prioritization
Say:

“There are many ways to apply AI. Which three opportunities have the highest ROI for this business right now?”

Now he must choose instead of rant.

5. Risk framing
Say:

“Moving fast is fine. Moving fast in the wrong direction creates expensive rework. I’d rather identify where AI adds real value than bolt it onto everything because it’s trendy.”

Strong but professional.

If He Says “AI Can Build This Easily”
Say:

“If by ‘build’ we mean generate a demo, yes often quickly. If we mean production-ready, maintainable, secure, integrated, observable, tested software inside an aging environment—that’s a different standard.”

If He Attacks You Personally or Implies Resistance
Say calmly:

messages.text:
“I’m not resisting AI. I’m separating marketing claims from implementation reality so we can make good decisions.”

If He Says “Others Are Doing It”
Say:

“Some are. Usually in greenfield systems, narrow scopes, or prototype environments. Our context matters.”

If He Rage-Quits or Makes Dramatic Statements
Do not chase.

Let silence happen.

Then say:

“I’m happy to continue constructively when we’re discussing specific deliverables and priorities.”

That makes you look senior.

Powerful Questions That Expose Weak Thinking
Use these selectively:

“Which current bottleneck do you believe AI solves first?”

“What budget or timeline reduction are we targeting?”

“Who owns validation when AI output is wrong?”

“How do we manage security/privacy with client data?”

“What existing system constraints are we deprioritizing to make room for this?”

These questions turn slogans into accountability.

Tone Strategy
Use:

Slow pace

Short sentences

No sarcasm

No emotional loading

Calm certainty

The calmer you are, the worse impulsive behavior looks.

What NOT to Do
Avoid:

“You don’t understand development.”

“That’s stupid.”

“You made wireframes, not software.”

“You’re childish.”

Even if true, those lines lose political capital.

The Highest-Impact Closing Statement
If needed:

“I’m fully supportive of modernizing how we work, including AI. My only concern is that we apply it where it creates measurable value rather than assuming it removes the complexity of an established production system.”

That sounds executive-level.

My Honest Read
You may already suspect he wants a scapegoat. If so, this call may not be about truth—it may be about narrative. Your job is to leave the agency thinking:

“He was the most professional person in the room.”

That often matters more than whether the client keeps posturing.

One More Important Note
If you go in trying to humiliate him, adrenaline can make you sloppy. If you go in trying to be undeniable, you’ll be sharper.

If you’d like, I can also help you build a live call script with likely attacks from him + perfect responses, based on your exact project and legacy stack.

messages.text:
Exactly. If you're already using Next.js App Router + ISR, that's probably the cleanest solution.

You can think of it as:

Users
  │
  ▼
Vercel CDN
  │
  ▼
ISR Cache
  │
  ▼
Postgres

instead of:

Users
  │
  ▼
Redis
  │
  ▼
Postgres
Example architecture
                 ADMIN
                   │
                   ▼
             Update data
                   │
                   ▼
               Postgres
                   │
                   ▼
      revalidateTag('list')
                   │
                   ▼

        Next.js ISR Cache
                   │
            cached JSON
                   │
                   ▼
              Vercel CDN
                   │
                   ▼
                Users
Endpoint
// app/api/list/route.ts

import { unstable_cache } from "next/cache";

const getList = unstable_cache(
  async () => {
    return db.list.findFirst();
  },
  ["list"],
  {
    revalidate: 86400, // 24h
    tags: ["list"],
  }
);

Request flow:

First request
    │
    ▼
Postgres
    │
    ▼
Cache result

Next million requests
    │
    ▼
Cache
Admin update
import { revalidateTag } from "next/cache";

await updateList(...);

revalidateTag("list");

Flow:

Admin changes data
      │
      ▼
Postgres updated
      │
      ▼
revalidateTag('list')
      │
      ▼
cache invalidated
      │
      ▼
next request rebuilds cache
User-side cache

You can still keep:

localStorage

for instant startup:

Open app
   │
   ▼
Read localStorage
   │
   ▼
Render instantly
   │
   ▼
Check version

but the server side becomes:

User
  │
  ▼
CDN
  │
  ▼
ISR Cache
  │
  ▼
Postgres
DB read frequency

In practice:

No updates:
    ~1 read per cache lifecycle

After update:
    1 read to rebuild cache

Flood attack:
    still served from cache

So for your requirement:

DB read should be max 1/24h unless manually refreshed

ISR + tag revalidation is very close to exactly what you want, without introducing Redis, KV, or another moving part. The only caveat is that under some edge cases (multiple regions, cache eviction, deployments), you should think of it as "approximately one rebuild per cache invalidation", not a strict cryptographic guarantee of one database query every 24 hours. For most applications, that's more than sufficient.
